title: How Tu Youyou figured it out
body:
Tu’s group compiled some 2,000 candidate recipes from classical texts and folk medicine, narrowed them to roughly 640 plant extracts, and tested each against Plasmodium berghei-infected mice. Qinghao, prescribed in Chinese medicine for intermittent fevers for some 1,500 years, showed real but maddeningly inconsistent activity: sometimes near-total inhibition, sometimes nothing at all.

The unlock came from a 4th-century manuscript. In Ge Hong’s Zhouhou Beijifang (“Handbook of Prescriptions for Emergencies”) Tu found a one-line preparation note for qinghao: “take a handful, soak in two sheng of water, wring out the juice, drink it all.” The crucial detail was the cold soak. Every other herbal preparation she had tried used boiling, and her own laboratory extraction was a hot ethanol reflux. If Ge Hong was right, the active ingredient was heat-labile and her solvent was destroying it. She switched to diethyl ether, which boils at 35°C; on 4 October 1971, sample #191 produced 100% inhibition in mouse and monkey malaria.

Tu and two colleagues then admitted themselves to the Beijing 309 Hospital and took the extract themselves, not as malaria patients (none of them were infected) but as healthy volunteers checking for acute toxicity before exposing infected patients to an unknown compound. A week of inpatient observation produced no adverse effects. The team then moved to genuine cases on Hainan Island, where qinghao extract cleared parasitemia in days.[[cite:lasker-artemisinin-2011]][[cite:tu-youyou-wikipedia]]
